title: Instance Methods 🏃
description: Teach your objects how to take action.

slides.title: Behavior 🏃
slides.content:
A Class defines State (Attributes) and **Behavior**.

We define Behavior using **Instance Methods**. These are just standard methods written inside the Class.

Instead of the whole program running it, the specific Object runs it.

slides.title: Teaching a Dog to Bark 🐕
slides.content: Notice how the `bark()` method doesn't have the word `static`. (We will explain `static` later in the course, but for normal OOP, omit `static`).

quizData.question: If we create `dogA` named 'Apollo' and `dogB` named 'Zeus', what happens if we only call `dogA.bark();`?
quizData.explanation: Methods are tied to the specific Object instance you call them on. Giving a command to `dogA` only activates `dogA`'s code.

slides.title: Self-Awareness 🧠
slides.content:
The best part about Instance Methods is that they are 'Self-Aware'.

When `myDog.bark()` runs, it automatically knows to print "Rex", because Java seamlessly links the method run to the specific Object's data.

If `yourDog.bark()` runs, it will print whatever name `yourDog` has.
